title: Custom Comparators
description: Writing your own comparison logic for sort() and priority_queue with a lambda, sorting pairs by any field, and the strict weak ordering rule.
objectives: Write a custom comparator lambda for sort()
Sort a vector of pairs by a specific field
Understand the strict weak ordering rule a comparator must follow
Fully understand priority_queue's comparator syntax, previewed earlier

sections.text: 1. Recap: greater<int>() Was Only the Beginning

Sorting using sort() showed greater<int>() for descending order. That's a ready-made comparator. Sometimes the sorting rule needed is more specific than "ascending" or "descending" — that's when a custom comparator, usually a lambda from Lambda Functions, comes in.
A comparator is a function (often a lambda) that takes two elements and returns true if the first should come before the second in the sorted order.

sort()'s comparator argument accepts any callable with this shape — a lambda, a named function, or a functor like greater<int>(). The lambda form is usually the most direct way to express custom logic right at the call site.
3. Sorting Pairs by a Specific Field

sections.title: What the comparator is really saying
points: a.second > b.second means: a should come before b when a's marks are higher
This ties directly to pair.first/.second from pair — sorting isn't limited to plain numbers
The default sort() has no idea what 'marks' means; the comparator is what teaches it the actual criteria

sections.text: A comparator must implement what's called a strict weak ordering — informally: it should return false when comparing an element to itself (comp(a, a) must be false), and it must be logically consistent (if a should come before b, then b should NOT come before a). Writing a broken comparator, like return a <= b; for a sort expecting strict less-than, can cause undefined behavior in sort() itself, not just wrong output.

Recall this line from priority_queue, left as a preview. Now it's fully explainable: priority_queue<T, Container, Comparator> — T is the element type (int), Container is what it's built on internally (vector<int>, the default), and Comparator (greater<int>) decides ordering, exactly like sort()'s third argument. Using greater<int> flips the default max-heap into a min-heap, because it reverses what 'comes first.'

sections.title: Telling the card sorter your own rule
sections.text: Recall sort()'s professional card sorter analogy. A custom comparator is you telling that sorter your own specific rule instead of the default "numerically ascending" — "sort these by suit, then by rank," or here, "sort these students by marks, highest first." The sorter still does all the actual work; you've just given it a precise rule to follow.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing a comparator using <= instead of < (or its equivalent) — breaks strict weak ordering and can cause undefined behavior in sort().
Forgetting the comparator's return type must be bool — true means 'the first argument should come before the second.'
Confusing which field to compare when sorting pairs or objects — double check .first vs .second, or the intended member, matches the actual sorting goal.
Assuming a comparator is only for sort() — the exact same idea configures priority_queue's ordering too.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a comparator, in the context of sort()?
questions.answer: A function (commonly a lambda) taking two elements and returning true if the first should come before the second in the sorted order.

questions.question: How would you sort a vector of pairs by their second element, in descending order?
questions.answer: sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](pair<A,B> a, pair<A,B> b) { return a.second > b.second; });

questions.question: What rule must a valid comparator follow?
questions.answer: Strict weak ordering — it must return false comparing an element to itself, and be logically consistent; violating this can cause undefined behavior in sort(), not just incorrect results.

questions.question: How does priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> work?
questions.answer: It's a priority_queue of int, built on a vector<int> internally, using greater<int> as its comparator — which flips the default max-heap behavior into a min-heap.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A custom comparator, usually a lambda, teaches sort() (or priority_queue) exactly what 'comes first' means for a specific case — for example, sorting a vector<pair<string,int>> by .second in descending order with [](auto a, auto b) { return a.second > b.second; }. A valid comparator must follow strict weak ordering: logically consistent, and false when comparing an element to itself — violating this causes undefined behavior in sort() itself. This is also exactly what powers priority_queue<T, Container, Comparator>, where swapping in greater<int> as the comparator turns the default max-heap into a min-heap.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Custom Comparators
sections.text: Comparator = your own rule handed to the card sorter. Returns true = 'a belongs before b.' Must be logically consistent — no contradictions allowed.
